key_insights.insight: Effective communication involves understanding and controlling the cues we send and receive, impacting our relationships, career, and confidence.
key_insights.quote: Very highly successful people speak a hidden language, and that is the language of cues. If you don't know how to read the cues people are sending to you, if you don't know how to control the cues you're sending to others, you are missing a crucial element of success.

key_insights.insight: Impressions are significantly shaped by warmth and competence cues, making it crucial to manage how we project these qualities to be memorable and confident.
key_insights.quote: 82% of our impressions of people are based on warmth and competence. That means that if we can control our warmth cues and our competence cues, we know we are taking care of 82% of our impression.

key_insights.insight: Our brains are wired to detect intention, and gestures, especially hand movements, play a vital role in signaling our intentions and trustworthiness to others.
key_insights.quote: Your brain is 12.5 times more likely to believe my gesture over my words. And that's because it is very hard to lie with our gestures.

key_insights.insight: Resting Bothered Face (RBF) is a real phenomenon where one's default facial expression can unintentionally signal sadness, anger, or fear, necessitating conscious effort to counteract these signals.
key_insights.quote: You need to look in the mirror and figure out what is the default of your face. If your mouth angles down into a frown, people are going to think you're sad. You're going to have to be counteracting that with your cues.

key_insights.insight: Using intentional word choices, even in something as simple as a calendar invite, can prime people to feel or think a certain way, setting them up for a more positive and collaborative interaction.
key_insights.quote: Those words are actually cuing that person's brain every single time they open their calendar, that when we read a word like collaborate, we are literally more likely to be collaborative.

key_insights.insight: Highly competent individuals can unintentionally appear suspicious if they do not demonstrate sufficient warmth cues.
key_insights.quote: Too much competence without enough warmth leaves people feeling suspicious. So no matter how competent you are, no matter how good your ideas are, if you are not showcasing that with warmth, people are skeptical of you.

key_insights.insight: Nonverbal bridges, such as leaning in, are great ways to build warmth and help you bridge or move closer to someone in a conversation.
key_insights.quote: Nonverbal bridges are when someone is trying to bridge the distance between you... it also could be a reach out.

key_insights.insight: When presenting, whether on stage or in a meeting, it is important to show purposeful movement instead of pacing or not moving. This will help the audience follow and categorize your talk more effectively.
key_insights.quote: You want to help your audience with your stage movement. So in my presentations, when I'm sharing science or fact or research, I'm on the left-hand side of the stage. When I'm moving to personal story, gifts, anecdotes, or a funny video, I'm on the right side of the stage.

actionable_advice.advice: Identify and strategically counteract your resting bothered face to manage first impressions.
actionable_advice.how_to: Look in the mirror to identify the default expression of your face. Actively engage your muscles to make it more 'upward' during initial meetings and interactions.

actionable_advice.advice: Stop asking “What do you do?”, and replace it with better open-ended questions to generate more engagement
actionable_advice.how_to: For 30 days, go on a “what do you do diet” and start asking, 'What exciting projects are you currently working on?' or 'What are your biggest goals right now?'

actionable_advice.advice: Practice and use the steeple hand gesture to project competence and confidence in professional settings.
actionable_advice.how_to: Bring your hands together in front of you, forming a triangle shape with your fingertips, keeping your palms relaxed and visible. Avoid drumming your fingers to prevent conveying nervousness.

actionable_advice.advice: Intentional Stage Movement to Communicate Meaning and Intent
actionable_advice.how_to: Plan out purposeful movement for the left, right, and center of the stage. This can help you communicate different parts of a speech or different emotions with an audience.

actionable_advice.advice: Learn to spot untrustworthy people by understanding signs of lying.
actionable_advice.how_to: When suspecting dishonesty, look for discrepancies between what is said and head movements, voice inflection, and face twitches.

memorable_stories: The story of Jamie Semenoff, founder of Ring, who failed to secure a deal on Shark Tank despite having a billion-dollar idea due to poor communication.
The experiment of skydiving sweat triggering the fear response in unsuspecting participants
Vanessa's own experience hugging an executive and destroying her TV show pitch
contrarian_claims: Charisma is not genetic but a learned skill that can be acquired by understanding and controlling social cues.
Extroverts are not the best people to teach communication as the skill is much easier for them to execute and harder for them to explain than introverts
Making more eye contact is bad advice because it can be taken as intimidating.
When it comes to first impression or first handshake, DO NOT use The Politician's Handshake (cupping hands).
episode_title: The Diary Of A CEO with Steven Bartlett - The Body Language Expert: Stop Using This, It’s Making People Dislike You, So Are These Subtle Mistakes! Your Resting Face Matters & How To Fix It!
